name: World War II - Pacific Theater / Asia-Pacific War
summary: This report treats the conflict as the December 1941-August/September 1945 Pacific War while noting that many China casualty sources cover the full 1937-1945 Sino-Japanese War. The death toll was dominated by Chinese military and civilian losses, Japanese military deaths, Japanese occupation-related famine and forced labor in Southeast Asia, Allied bombing of Japan, and the Bengal famine. A defensible broad range is about 20-30 million deaths, depending on whether full China 1937-45 and wider famine/occupation mortality are included.

sides.name: Empire of Japan / Axis-aligned side
members: collaborationist regimes in occupied China and Southeast Asia

military_killed.notes: Japanese military/service deaths including combat, disease, starvation, ship sinkings, missing presumed dead and other service-related causes. The lower figure is the common WWII military-dead table estimate; the higher figure follows broader Japanese government-related categories for military/quasi-military war dead.

breakdown.cause: massacre, executions, anti-guerrilla operations, air attacks
breakdown.notes: Very uncertain. Includes direct killings in China; some sources cite Rummel-derived estimates of millions directly murdered by Japanese forces, while Chinese wartime categories separate only some direct deaths.
Often cited from historian Mitsuyoshi Himeta; blends direct killing with destruction, displacement and deprivation.
Total civilian deaths in the Battle of Manila were about 100,000; many were attributed to Japanese massacres, while some were caused by U.S. artillery and urban combat.

breakdown.victims_group: Ethnic Chinese civilians in Singapore and Malaya
breakdown.notes: Sook Ching estimates range from Japanese records around 5,000-6,000 to retrospective/historical estimates around 25,000-50,000, with some higher Singaporean claims.

breakdown.victims_group: Chinese, Korean, Mongolian, Soviet/Russian and Allied prisoners and civilians
breakdown.notes: At least 3,000 deaths are documented for Unit 731 human experimentation; broader biological-warfare death estimates in China range roughly 200,000-500,000 and are highly uncertain.

civilians_killed_directly.notes: Direct civilian killings by Japanese forces were massive but cannot be cleanly separated from indirect occupation deaths in many regions. The low/high range is intentionally broad.

deaths_from_actions.victims_group: Southeast Asian civilians under Japanese occupation
deaths_from_actions.notes: Gregg Huff/Cambridge estimates that famine and forced labor accounted for most of about 4.4-4.5 million Southeast Asian civilian deaths under Japanese occupation.
Includes famine, forced labor and occupation breakdown; the 3.3 million point refers to a demographic estimate of net population loss in Java during Japanese occupation.

The 1944-45 Vietnam famine was caused by Japanese occupation demands, French colonial administration, transport disruption, Allied attacks and crop failures; attribution is shared but Japanese requisition and occupation policy were central.

deaths_from_actions.victims_group: Allied POWs and Asian rōmusha on the Burma-Thailand Railway
deaths_from_actions.notes: Many standard accounts give about 90,000 Asian civilian laborer deaths and about 12,000-16,000 Allied POW deaths. These overlap with broader Southeast Asia occupation-death estimates.

deaths_from_actions.victims_group: Western Allied prisoners of war in Japanese custody
deaths_from_actions.notes: Approximately 132,000 Western Allied POWs were captured by Japan; about 27% died. Some aggregate POW-death figures differ depending on inclusion of deaths from Allied attacks on POW transport ships.

sides.name: Allies
members: Free French and other Allied forces

military_killed.notes: Dominated by China. China is commonly listed at 3-4 million military deaths for the 1937-45 war with Japan/WWII. Additional deaths include roughly 108,000-112,000 U.S. Asia-Pacific deaths, about 57,000 Philippine military deaths, roughly 30,000 British Empire Far East deaths, Soviet killed/missing in 1945 operations, and smaller Dutch/Commonwealth losses.

breakdown.notes: USSBS Medical Division estimated 333,000 killed and 473,000 wounded by bombing of Japan; other USSBS survey approaches produced higher estimates up to about 900,000 killed with large sampling error.

breakdown.victims_group: Civilians and military personnel in Hiroshima and Nagasaki
breakdown.notes: RERF estimates 90,000-166,000 deaths in Hiroshima and 60,000-80,000 in Nagasaki by the end of 1945. These deaths are included within the broader strategic-bombing category and should not be double counted with it.
Operation Meetinghouse killed at least 80,000 and likely more than 100,000 in one night. These deaths are included in the broader bombing-of-Japan total.

Total Okinawan civilian deaths are often estimated at 40,000-150,000, frequently 100,000-150,000. Attribution between U.S. firepower, Japanese coercion, mass suicide, starvation and crossfire is not cleanly separable.
About 100,000 civilians died in Manila; one estimate attributes up to 40% of noncombatant deaths to U.S. military operations. This is not a firm count.

civilians_killed_directly.notes: The lower bound counts only USSBS Medical Division deaths from bombing of Japan. The upper bound includes high USSBS survey estimates and some civilian deaths from Okinawa/Manila attributable to Allied firepower; overlap and attribution uncertainty are severe.

deaths_from_actions.victims_group: Bengal civilians under British Indian wartime administration
deaths_from_actions.notes: The Bengal famine death toll is commonly summarized around 2.1 million in scholarly consensus, with wider estimates up to 3.8 million. British wartime policy was a major causal factor, but not the sole cause; Japanese conquest of Burma, transport disruption, inflation, market failure, crop issues and colonial administrative failures all contributed.

deaths_from_actions.victims_group: Japanese civilians affected by blockade and shipping collapse
deaths_from_actions.notes: The Allied submarine campaign and mining/blockade severely disrupted food and fuel flows, but I did not find a reliable separable death toll attributable specifically to blockade-induced civilian starvation in Japan.

totals.notes: The military total combines roughly 3.2-4.3 million Allied military deaths with 2.12-2.3 million Japanese military deaths. The civilian range is a cautious synthesis of direct killings, bombing deaths, occupation famine/forced labor, China famine/disease and Bengal famine; it should not be treated as a precise arithmetic sum because major sources overlap and use different date boundaries. Grand total is rounded to about 20-30 million.

key_atrocities.name: Strategic bombing of Japan
key_atrocities.notes: USSBS Medical Division estimated 333,000 killed; other survey estimates were higher.

key_atrocities.name: Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki
key_atrocities.victims_group: Japanese civilians and military personnel in target cities
key_atrocities.notes: RERF estimates deaths by the end of 1945 at 90,000-166,000 in Hiroshima and 60,000-80,000 in Nagasaki.

key_atrocities.name: Tokyo firebombing / Operation Meetinghouse
key_atrocities.notes: Often described as the deadliest single air raid in history; about one million were left homeless.

key_atrocities.name: Japanese occupation of Southeast Asia
key_atrocities.notes: Mostly famine and forced labor deaths under occupation.

key_atrocities.name: Vietnam famine
key_atrocities.perpetrator: Empire of Japan and Vichy/French colonial administration, with Allied transport attacks as contributing factor
key_atrocities.notes: Attribution is shared, but Japanese requisition and occupation policy were central.

key_atrocities.name: Java/Indonesia famine and romusha forced labor
key_atrocities.notes: Includes famine, forced labor and occupation-related mortality; a demographic study estimates 3.3 million net population loss in Java during occupation.

key_atrocities.name: Manila massacre / Battle of Manila
key_atrocities.perpetrator: Empire of Japan, with additional deaths from Allied urban combat
key_atrocities.notes: About 100,000 civilians died; Japanese massacres were central, but U.S. artillery and urban combat also caused civilian deaths.

key_atrocities.name: Battle of Okinawa civilian deaths
key_atrocities.perpetrator: Allies / United States and Empire of Japan
key_atrocities.notes: Civilian deaths resulted from crossfire, U.S. bombardment, Japanese coercion, mass suicide, starvation and battlefield collapse.

key_atrocities.name: Three Alls policy
key_atrocities.notes: Commonly cited from Mitsuyoshi Himeta; includes blended direct and indirect deaths.

key_atrocities.name: Bengal famine
key_atrocities.perpetrator: British imperial wartime administration, with Japanese conquest of Burma and wartime disruption as contributing causes
key_atrocities.notes: Attribution remains debated; British wartime policy and colonial administrative failures were major factors but not the only causes.

uncertainty_notes: The largest uncertainty is the treatment of China: many figures cover 1937-1945 rather than the 1941-1945 Pacific War. Direct Japanese civilian killings are hard to separate from famine, disease, scorched-earth policy and displacement. Several categories overlap, especially Southeast Asian occupation deaths, forced labor deaths and country totals. Bengal is included for fairness because it was a major Asia wartime famine shaped by British wartime policy, but its inclusion in a narrow Pacific Theater definition is debatable.
